senses.definition: Medical examination of the inside of the abdomen using an endoscope to look for disease.
examples: The surgeon recommended abdominoscopy to investigate the unexplained abdominal pain.
Modern abdominoscopy allows doctors to see the abdominal organs without making a large cut.
Abdominoscopy is often performed under general anaesthesia in a hospital.

senses.definition: Relating to both the abdomen and the thorax or chest.
examples: The abdominothoracic region includes the lower chest and upper abdominal area.
He suffered from an abdominothoracic injury after the car crash.
Abdominothoracic surgery can be complex and requires special training.

senses.definition: Having a large or sticking‑out belly.
examples: The abdominous cat lay lazily in the sun.
An increasingly abdominous figure may be a sign of poor diet and lack of exercise.
The novelist described the king as short, abdominous, and red‑faced.

senses.definition: Relating to both the abdomen and the vagina.
examples: The operation required an abdominovaginal surgical approach.
Certain abdominovaginal procedures are used in gynaecology.
The patient showed signs of abdominovaginal pain after the treatment.

senses.definition: Relating to both the abdomen and the urinary bladder.
examples: The surgeon used an abdominovesical route to reach the bladder.
Abdominovesical injuries can be life‑threatening if not treated quickly.
An abdominovesical incision was made to remove the tumour.

senses.definition: To take something or someone away, or to lead them away from somewhere.
examples: The ancient text says that evil spirits could abduce a person from their home.
In physiology, a muscle can abduce a limb from the body.
The magician seemed to abduce the coin from the boy's hand.

senses.definition: To use facts as evidence for an explanation or conclusion.
examples: From these observations, we can abduce that the theory is wrong.
The detective tried to abduce a motive from the suspect's behaviour.
Scientists abduce possible causes from the data they collect.

senses.definition: Past tense and past participle of abduce.
examples: The limb was abduced by the contraction of the muscle.
The philosopher abduced his theory from a series of simple observations.

senses.definition: The sixth cranial nerve, which controls the muscle that moves the eye outward.
examples: Damage to the abducens can cause double vision.
The doctor tested the patient's abducens function by asking him to look sideways.
Abducens palsy often results in difficulty moving the eye laterally.

senses.definition: Carrying away from the midline or centre of the body.
examples: The abducent fibres of the nerve control outward eye movement.
An abducent muscle moves a limb away from the body.
Abducent pathways are involved in lateral eye movements.

senses.definition: Another name for the sixth cranial nerve, which moves the eye outward.
examples: The abducent was affected, so the patient's eye could not turn outwards.
A tumour compressed the abducent on the right side.

senses.definition: Plural form of abducent, especially referring to both sixth cranial nerves.
examples: Diseases such as meningitis can affect the abducentes.
The scan showed that both abducentes were intact.

senses.definition: Third person singular present of abduce.
examples: The lateral rectus muscle abduces the eye.
This nerve abduces the limb during movement.

senses.definition: Present participle of abduce.
examples: The muscle is abducing the leg away from the body.
Abducing the shoulder too quickly can cause injury.

senses.definition: To take someone away illegally by using force or tricks.
examples: The child was abducted on her way home from school.
The gang planned to abduct the wealthy businessman.
He was abducted by rebels and held for several weeks.

senses.definition: In anatomy, to move a limb or other body part away from the centre of the body.
examples: The therapist asked him to abduct his arm slowly.
This muscle helps to abduct the leg at the hip joint.
You may feel pain when you abduct the shoulder.

senses.definition: Past tense and past participle of abduct.
examples: The tourists were abducted by pirates near the coast.
She was abducted as a child but later found safe.

senses.definition: A person who has been abducted or kidnapped.
examples: The abductee was rescued after three days in captivity.
Families of the abductees gathered to demand action.
Each abductee received counselling after the incident.

senses.definition: Plural of abductee.
examples: Several abductees were released after negotiations.
The government promised support for the families of the abductees.

senses.definition: Present participle of abduct.
examples: The gang was arrested while abducting a businessman.
Abducting children is a serious crime in every country.

senses.definition: The act of taking someone away illegally, especially by using force.
examples: The police are investigating the abduction of a local schoolgirl.
Child abduction is punishable by a long prison sentence.
The film tells the story of an alien abduction.

senses.definition: In anatomy, movement of a limb or other body part away from the centre of the body.
examples: The exercise involves abduction of the hip.
Shoulder abduction may be limited after an injury.

senses.definition: Plural of abduction.
examples: There has been a rise in child abductions in recent years.
Reports of alien abductions are often questioned by scientists.

senses.definition: A person who abducts someone.
examples: The police arrested the child's abductor.
The abductors demanded a large ransom.
Her abductor was caught at the border.

senses.definition: In anatomy, a muscle that moves a limb or other body part away from the centre of the body.
examples: Weakness in the hip abductors can cause knee problems.
The therapist focused on strengthening the shoulder abductors.

senses.definition: Latin‑based plural referring to abductor muscles.
examples: The abductores of the hip are important for walking.
In anatomy texts, the term abductores may still appear.

senses.definition: Plural of abductor.
examples: The abductors released their hostages after negotiations.
Strong hip abductors help to stabilise the pelvis.

senses.definition: Third person singular present of abduct.
examples: The criminal abducts children from busy streets.
The lateral rectus muscle abducts the eye.

senses.definition: A male given name, often used as a short form of Abraham.
examples: Abe invited us all to his birthday party.
Her grandfather, Abe, was born in the 1930s.
Abe is short for Abraham.

senses.definition: At a right angle to the middle of a ship or aircraft, or directly to its side.
examples: The lighthouse was abeam on our starboard side.
The ship passed with the island abeam.
The aircraft reported being abeam the runway.

senses.definition: To bear, endure, or tolerate something.
examples: I can no longer abear this noise.
She could hardly abear the pain.

senses.definition: The act of bearing, enduring, or tolerating something.
examples: His quiet abearance of suffering moved them all.
Such insults are beyond abearance.

senses.definition: The act of bearing or supporting something.
examples: The pillars were for the abearing of the roof.
Such a weight is beyond the abearing of this old bridge.

senses.definition: Third person singular present of abear.
examples: He abears his troubles without complaint.
She abears the heat better than the rest of us.

senses.definition: A book or artwork based on the alphabet, often presenting words or images for each letter.
examples: The artist created an abecedaire of surreal objects.
This children's abecedaire pairs each letter with a colourful picture.
The museum displayed a medieval abecedaire used for teaching.

senses.definition: Plural of abecedarium; alphabet lists or books used to show or teach the letters.
examples: Archaeologists discovered several stone abecedaria at the site.
The library has a collection of medieval abecedaria.

senses.definition: A person who is learning the alphabet or the basics of a subject.
examples: The course is designed for abecedarians in computer science.
As an abecedarian in French, he still struggles with simple phrases.
The teacher patiently guided her abecedarians through each letter.

senses.definition: Relating to the alphabet or arranged in alphabetical order.
examples: The glossary is in abecedarian order.
An abecedarian list of authors appears at the end of the book.

senses.definition: Very simple or basic.
examples: The book offers an abecedarian introduction to physics.
Her knowledge of the subject is still abecedarian.

senses.definition: Plural of abecedarian.
examples: The teacher arranged different activities for the abecedarians.
The workshop welcomed abecedarians as well as more advanced students.

senses.definition: Plural of abecedary; alphabet books or lists showing letters in order.
examples: The exhibition displayed Renaissance abecedaries for children.
Many early abecedaries used religious texts.

senses.definition: An inscription, list, or book that shows the letters of an alphabet in order, often for practice or teaching.
examples: The stone abecedarium contains the complete Greek alphabet.
Children traced the letters in a wooden abecedarium.
The museum's abecedarium illustrates early writing practice.

senses.definition: A poem or text in which each line or section begins with successive letters of the alphabet.
examples: The psalm is an abecedarius, with each verse starting with the next letter.
Medieval monks often composed abecedarii to aid memorisation.

senses.definition: A book or other work that teaches or displays the alphabet.
examples: The child learned her letters from a colourful abecedary.
Collectors pay high prices for early printed abecedaries.
Each page of the abecedary shows a letter and a picture.

senses.definition: In bed, especially lying down.
examples: She was still abed at noon.
He lay abed, too sick to move.
On Sundays they often stay abed late.

senses.definition: A rare or obsolete spelling variant with no established separate meaning in modern English.
examples: The word abede appears only in a few historical texts.
Modern dictionaries usually do not list abede as a standard entry.

senses.definition: Along the edge or border of something.
examples: They walked abedge the frozen river.
The houses were built abedge the forest.

senses.definition: Obsolete form of beg, meaning to ask for charity or help.
examples: He went from door to door to abegge his bread.
The poor widow was forced to abegge in the streets.

senses.definition: Going begging; remaining unwanted or unused.
examples: We have several free tickets going abegging.
There is plenty of food abegging in the kitchen.
Opportunities were left abegging due to poor planning.

senses.definition: At a distance; away.
examples: He stood abeigh and watched in silence.
The village lay abeigh from the main road.

senses.definition: In the Bible, the second son of Adam and Eve, who was killed by his brother Cain.
examples: The story of Cain and Abel is well known in many cultures.
Abel offered a sacrifice that pleased God.

senses.definition: A male given name.
examples: Abel moved to the city when he was twenty.
My friend Abel is learning English.

senses.definition: A kind of poplar tree with pale leaves, also called the white poplar.
examples: An abele grew beside the river.
The garden was shaded by tall abeles.
They rested under an old abele at the edge of the field.

senses.definition: Plural of abele.
examples: A line of abeles marked the boundary of the estate.
The wind whispered through the leaves of the abeles.

senses.definition: A garden shrub with small, often fragrant flowers, grown as an ornamental plant.
examples: They planted abelia along the garden path.
The abelia blooms attract bees in late summer.
White and pink abelia varieties are popular in warm climates.

senses.definition: In mathematics, describing a group or structure in which the order of combining elements does not change the result.
examples: Addition of whole numbers forms an abelian group.
In an abelian group, a × b equals b × a.
The course includes a section on abelian and non‑abelian groups.

senses.definition: Plural of abelia.
examples: Several abelias were planted along the driveway.
The garden features roses, abelias, and lavender.

senses.definition: A rare or regional plant name, used in specialist or local contexts.
examples: The botanist noted the presence of abelicea in the area.
Abelicea is mentioned only in older botanical works.
